title: Helenium caspium
taxonomy: Helenium caspium is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the sunflower or daisy family. This family consists of flowering plants, often characterized by their composite flower heads. Helenium caspium, along with other members of the Asteraceae family, is further categorized into the subfamily Asteroideae. Within the subfamily, Helenium caspium is placed in the tribe Helenieae. The species Helenium caspium is also known for its specific classification within the genus Helenium.
uses: Helenium caspium, also known as golden ragwort, has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. Its roots and leaves are rich in flavonoids and other bioactive compounds that are said to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. In folk medicine, it has been used to treat a range of ailments, including digestive issues, skin problems, and respiratory infections. The plant's extracts have also been studied for their potential antioxidant and immunomodulatory effects. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of Helenium caspium as a medicinal herb.

intro: Helenium caspium, also known as the Caspian Sneezeweed, is a perennial plant species belonging to the sunflower family, characterized by its bright yellow flowers and medicinal properties. The plant has been traditionally used in medicine to treat various health issues, including inflammation and respiratory problems, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Helenium caspium include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its therapeutic effects. In herbal medicine, the plant is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat colds, coughs, and other respiratory issues.
